did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning models (ResNet, EfficientNet, and MobileNetV2) to classify skin conditions (Acrochordon, Lichenoid Keratosis, and Vascular Lesions) from the ISIC dataset. The results, including accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score, were obtained by training and evaluating these AI models.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results comparing the performance of ResNet, EfficientNet, and MobileNetV2 models on the ISIC dataset for skin condition detection. It includes specific methodology (data preparation, training with 100 epochs), quantitative results (accuracy, recall, precision, F1-score), and new findings concluding that ResNet and EfficientNet outperform MobileNetV2. Although it mentions reviewing 15 research papers in the introduction, the primary contribution is the empirical validation of deep learning models, classifying it as a research paper (specifically a conference paper).

title: Comparative Analysis of ResNet, EfficientNet, and MobileNetV2 for Detecting Skin Conditions in Deep Learning Models
journal: Proceedings of the International Conference on Sustainable Communication Networks and Application (ICSCNA-2024)
brief_summary: This study compares the effectiveness of ResNet, EfficientNet, and MobileNetV2 deep learning models for detecting three specific skin conditions: Acrochordon, Lichenoid Keratosis, and Vascular Lesions. The researchers utilized the ISIC dataset, which was augmented via Roboflow to create a final set of 2,127 images split into training, validation, and test sets. Each model was trained for 100 epochs using pre-trained ImageNet weights, with performance evaluated on accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score. The results showed that both ResNet and EfficientNet achieved a superior accuracy of 92.1875%, outperforming MobileNetV2, which achieved 90.625% accuracy. Additionally, EfficientNet demonstrated the highest recall (93%) and precision (93%) among the three models. The study concludes that ResNet and EfficientNet are significantly better suited for accurately detecting these skin conditions compared to MobileNetV2.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three skin conditions studied; 269 images collected from the ISIC dataset.
One of the three skin conditions studied; 273 images collected from the ISIC dataset.
One of the three skin conditions studied; 347 images collected from the ISIC dataset.

all_task_types.target: Discrete skin condition labels (Acrochordon, Lichenoid Keratosis, Vascular Lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the research is about 'classification of skin conditions' using ResNet, EfficientNet, and MobileNetV2. The models assign images to one of three predefined classes (Acrochordon, Lichenoid Keratosis, Vascular Lesions) from the ISIC dataset. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics: accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score, along with normalized confusion matrices.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)
datasets_used.description: An open-source dataset from which images were handpicked and manually gathered. The original collection consisted of 889 images, which were then augmented using Roboflow to create a final dataset of 2,127 images.

use.training: Used for training ResNet, EfficientNet, and MobileNetV2 models for 100 epochs.
use.validation: Used as a validation set (9% of total) to provide unbiased evaluation during training.
use.testing: Used as a test set (4% of total) to evaluate model performance and calculate metrics like accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score.
use.other: Original 889 images were augmented to generate the final 2,127 images.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the proportion of correct and incorrect predictions across classes and check for class imbalance by normalizing values between 0 and 1.
To quantitatively compare the classification performance of ResNet, EfficientNet, and MobileNetV2 models on the test set.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared model performance using descriptive statistics (accuracy, recall, precision, F1-score) and normalized confusion matrices without statistical inference.

ISIC Dataset (Augmented, 2127 images).experiment: Classification of Acrochordon, Lichenoid Keratosis, and Vascular Lesions
ISIC Dataset (Augmented, 2127 images).notes: ResNet and EfficientNet achieved the highest accuracy (92.1875%). EfficientNet demonstrated the highest recall (93%) and precision (93%) among the three models. MobileNetV2 had the lowest performance across all metrics but required the least training time. Normalized confusion matrices were used to evaluate class imbalance and prediction proportions.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models with weights from ImageNet were used; pre-trained layers were frozen.
architecture_used.input_size: Resized and normalized to fit the model's input requirements (specific dimensions not reported).

architecture_details.ResNet: Deep convolutional neural network applying residual learning to improve accuracy and avoid vanishing gradient problem.
architecture_details.EfficientNet: Model that scales dimensions (width, depth, resolution) in a balanced manner to achieve high accuracy using fewer parameters.
architecture_details.MobileNetV2: Efficient, lightweight convolutional neural network designed for high accuracy with less computational resources.

training_details.hyperparameters: Learning rate, batch size, and number of epochs mentioned as tunable parameters in suggestions, but specific values used for training not reported.
training_details.training_schedule: Models trained on 2127 images (1857 train, 182 validation, 88 test).

classification_or_output_head.details: Assigns image to one of the predefined classes based on learned patterns.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation using Roboflow to increase dataset size from 889 to 2127 images
Image resizing to fit model input requirements
Image normalization to fit model input requirements
Dataset splitting into training (87%), validation (9%), and test (4%) sets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The dataset was labeled and augmented using Roboflow. The original collection of 889 images from the ISIC dataset was augmented to create a final set of 2,127 images. Specific geometric or color transform parameters (e.g., rotation angles, flip probabilities) were not reported in the text.

The paper suggests adopting sophisticated augmentation techniques like rotation, zoom, flip, brightness, and contrast adjustments in the future to improve robustness, but these were not explicitly stated as being applied during the reported experiments.

conclusions: ResNet and EfficientNet models achieved a superior accuracy of 92.1875% in detecting Acrochordon, Lichenoid Keratosis, and Vascular Lesions, outperforming MobileNetV2 which achieved 90.625% accuracy.
EfficientNet demonstrated the highest recall (93%) and precision (93%) among the three models, indicating superior performance in correctly identifying true positives and minimizing false positives.
ResNet and EfficientNet are significantly better suited for accurately detecting the studied skin conditions compared to MobileNetV2.

MobileNetV2 required the least training time (97.68 minutes) compared to EfficientNet (144.5 minutes) and ResNet (258.48 minutes), but had the lowest performance metrics across accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score.

The study concludes that while ResNet and EfficientNet provide high accuracy for skin condition detection, there is scope for further refinement in performance, reliability, and efficiency through techniques such as data augmentation, hyperparameter tuning, ensemble learning, and handling class imbalance.
important_remarks: The test set is extremely small (88 images, 4% of total), which limits the statistical power and reliability of the reported performance metrics.
The dataset was manually handpicked and gathered from the ISIC archive rather than using a standard, fixed split, introducing potential selection bias.
Data augmentation was performed using Roboflow, but specific augmentation techniques (e.g., rotation, flip, zoom) and their parameters were not reported.

The study focuses on three rare or specific skin conditions (Acrochordon, Lichenoid Keratosis, Vascular Lesions) rather than common skin cancers (e.g., melanoma), limiting generalizability to broader dermatological screening.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance differences between models.
Hyperparameters such as learning rate, batch size, and optimizer were not reported, making the experiment difficult to reproduce exactly.
The paper suggests future work for cross-validation, ensemble learning, and handling class imbalance, indicating these were not addressed in the current study.
The test set split (4%) is unusually low for deep learning evaluation, raising concerns about the robustness of the accuracy estimates.
